natural_occurrence.description: A synthetic azo dye derived from coal tar and petroleum-based aromatic compounds. Not known to occur naturally. Produces red to burgundy colours approximately 100 times more intense than natural beetroot extract.

manufacturing.description: Synthesized through diazotization of naphthalene-4-sulfonic acid, followed by azo coupling with 1-naphthol-4-sulfonic acid. The resulting azo compound is converted to the disodium salt form, purified, and standardized to food-grade specifications.

other_uses.cosmetics: Used in EU-approved cosmetics (lipsticks, eyeshadows) but restricted in some jurisdictions
other_uses.industrial: Historically used as textile dye before food applications

eu.max_level_mg_kg: varies by food category (100 mg/kg in beverages, 200 mg/kg in confectionery)
eu.conditions: Must carry warning: 'May have an adverse effect on activity and attention in children' (since 2010)

usa.notes: Never authorized for use in foods, drugs, or cosmetics. Import of products containing E122 prohibited.

japan.notes: Approved as synthetic colour with purity specifications and maximum use levels

canada.notes: Permitted in specific food categories with maximum use levels

regulatory_history.event: Approved for use in UK and European countries as coal tar dye
JECFA established ADI of 0-4 mg/kg body weight per day
Southampton Study published linking E122 and five other colours (the 'Southampton Six') to hyperactivity in children
EFSA re-evaluated E122, maintained ADI but acknowledged behavioural concerns in sensitive children
EU mandated warning label: 'May have an adverse effect on activity and attention in children'
Major UK retailers (M&S, Waitrose) voluntarily removed Southampton Six colours including E122, triggering industry-wide reformulation

data_sources: McCann et al. (2007) Southampton Study, The Lancet

commonly_found_in: Strawberry and raspberry flavoured desserts (blancmange, mousses)
Red and pink jams, jellies, and preserves
Breadcrumbs and coating mixes for processed meats

equivalents.product: litres of soft drink (at 100mg/kg max level)

adi_context.source_note: Based on EFSA ADI (2009) and maximum permitted levels in EU Regulation 1333/2008. Actual usage levels are typically lower than maximum permitted levels.
